text: What is the purpose of an index in MongoDB?
options: To enforce uniqueness on a field
To speed up read queries by avoiding full collection scans
correctAnswer: To speed up read queries by avoiding full collection scans
explanation: Indexes speed up reads by allowing MongoDB to find matching documents without scanning every document. The trade-off: indexes slow down writes (each index must be updated).
hint: Same purpose as in PostgreSQL — speed up SELECT.

text: Create an ascending index on the 'publishedYear' field:
correctAnswer: createIndex
explanation: createIndex() creates an index. 1 = ascending, -1 = descending. For most single-field queries, direction doesn't matter for performance (only for sort optimization).
hint: It's one method that 'creates' an 'Index'.

text: What does 'COLLSCAN' mean in MongoDB's explain() output?
options: The query performed a full collection scan (no index used)
The query used a collation index
correctAnswer: The query performed a full collection scan (no index used)
explanation: COLLSCAN = Collection Scan — MongoDB read every document to find matches. This is slow for large collections. You want to see IXSCAN (index scan) instead.
hint: Like Seq Scan in PostgreSQL EXPLAIN.
sqlEquivalent: Seq Scan in PostgreSQL EXPLAIN ANALYZE

text: What happens to the existing data when you create an index on a collection that already has documents?
options: MongoDB indexes the existing documents automatically during index creation
Existing documents are not indexed — only new ones
correctAnswer: MongoDB indexes the existing documents automatically during index creation
explanation: MongoDB builds the index over all existing documents when you call createIndex(). This may take time for large collections but existing data is fully indexed.
hint: MongoDB handles backfilling existing data.

text: How do you create a unique index to prevent duplicate email addresses?
correctAnswer: db.users.createIndex({ email: 1 }, { unique: true })
explanation: Pass { unique: true } as the options object to createIndex() to enforce uniqueness. Attempting to insert a duplicate will throw a duplicate key error.
hint: Options are the second argument.

text: Which method lists all indexes on a collection?
correctAnswer: db.books.getIndexes()
explanation: getIndexes() returns an array of all index documents for the collection. You'll always see at least one: the default index on _id.
hint: Starts with 'get'.
sqlEquivalent: \d books in psql (shows indexes in table description)

text: What does explain('executionStats') provide that plain explain() doesn't?
options: Actual execution time and document counts from running the query
correctAnswer: Actual execution time and document counts from running the query
explanation: explain() gives you the query plan without executing. explain('executionStats') actually executes the query and returns real stats: execution time, documents examined, documents returned.
hint: 'executionStats' = actually ran the query.
sqlEquivalent: EXPLAIN = plan only; EXPLAIN ANALYZE = plan + actual execution stats

text: What is a compound index?
options: An index that covers multiple collections
An index built on multiple fields in a single document
correctAnswer: An index built on multiple fields in a single document
explanation: A compound index covers multiple fields: db.books.createIndex({ authorId: 1, publishedYear: -1 }). It can speed up queries on authorId alone, or authorId + publishedYear together.
hint: Compound = multiple fields.
sqlEquivalent: CREATE INDEX idx ON books (author_id, published_year DESC)

text: Which index is automatically created on every MongoDB collection?
options: An index on the first field
A text index on all string fields
correctAnswer: An index on _id
explanation: MongoDB automatically creates a unique index on the _id field for every collection. This index cannot be dropped.
hint: Every document needs a unique _id.

text: In explain('executionStats'), what does a low ratio of nReturned to totalDocsExamined indicate?
options: The query is inefficient — scanning many docs to return few results (needs an index)
The query has a syntax error
correctAnswer: The query is inefficient — scanning many docs to return few results (needs an index)
explanation: Ideal: totalDocsExamined ≈ nReturned (examined only what was returned). Bad: totalDocsExamined = 10000, nReturned = 3 (reading 10000 docs to return 3 = needs an index).
hint: High examination, low return = wasted work = missing index.

text: You have a compound index on { authorId: 1, publishedYear: -1 }. Which query can use this index?
correctAnswer: db.books.find({ authorId: ObjectId('...') })
explanation: Compound indexes follow the 'prefix rule' — a query can use the index if it includes a prefix of the index fields. authorId is the prefix here, so a query on just authorId can use the index. publishedYear alone cannot.
hint: Think about left-to-right prefix matching.
sqlEquivalent: Same prefix rule applies to multi-column indexes in PostgreSQL

text: Create a sparse index on 'promoCode' (only index documents where the field exists):
correctAnswer: sparse
explanation: A sparse index only includes documents that have the indexed field. Great for optional fields — avoids storing null entries in the index.
hint: The option is called 'sparse'.

text:
Run this query with explain to check if it uses an index:
db.books.find({ genres: 'fiction' }).explain('executionStats')

What stage do you expect to see before creating an index on genres?
correctAnswer: COLLSCAN
explanation: Without an index on genres, you'll see stage: 'COLLSCAN'. After createIndex({ genres: 1 }), you'd see stage: 'IXSCAN'.
hint: Run it and look at queryPlanner.winningPlan.stage

text: What is a TTL (Time-To-Live) index used for?
options: Automatically deleting documents after a certain time
correctAnswer: Automatically deleting documents after a certain time
explanation: A TTL index automatically deletes documents when the indexed date field is older than expireAfterSeconds. Example: { expireAfterSeconds: 3600 } deletes documents 1 hour after their createdAt date.
hint: Documents 'expire' like session data or log entries.

text: What does 'IXSCAN' followed by 'FETCH' mean in an explain() plan?
options: Index was used to find document locations, then full documents were fetched from disk
The query was too complex for a single scan
An error occurred during index scan
correctAnswer: Index was used to find document locations, then full documents were fetched from disk
explanation: IXSCAN finds matching document pointers from the index, then FETCH retrieves the full documents. An index that covers all queried/projected fields can skip FETCH entirely (a 'covered query' — very fast).
hint: IXSCAN finds pointers, FETCH gets the documents.

text: Create a text index on both 'title' and 'tags' fields:
correctAnswer: 'text'|'text'
explanation: Text indexes use the string 'text' as the direction (not 1 or -1). You can have only one text index per collection, but it can cover multiple fields.
hint: Text indexes use the word 'text' not 1 or -1.
sqlEquivalent: CREATE INDEX idx ON books USING gin(to_tsvector('english', title || ' ' || tags))

text: What is the 'ESR Rule' for compound index field ordering?
options: Equality-Sort-Range — equality filters first, then sort fields, then range filters
correctAnswer: Equality-Sort-Range — equality filters first, then sort fields, then range filters
explanation: ESR: Put equality fields first (e.g., authorId = X), sort fields second (publishedYear ASC/DESC), range filters last (price < 20). This ordering maximizes index efficiency.
hint: E = exact match, S = sort, R = range.

text: Translate: CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_session ON sessions (session_token);
correctAnswer: db.sessions.createIndex({ sessionToken: 1 }, { unique: true })
explanation: createIndex with { unique: true } enforces uniqueness. Note: ensureIndex() is deprecated — always use createIndex().
hint: Pass { unique: true } as options.
sqlEquivalent: CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_session ON sessions (session_token)

text: What is a 'covered query' in MongoDB?
options: A query that uses a covered aggregate
A query where all queried and projected fields are in the index — no document fetch needed
A query covered by a text index
A query that covers all documents
correctAnswer: A query where all queried and projected fields are in the index — no document fetch needed
explanation: A covered query never reads the documents themselves — all data comes from the index. This is extremely fast. Example: find({ authorId: X }, { title: 1, _id: 0 }) covered by { authorId: 1, title: 1 }.
hint: The index 'covers' all needed data — no FETCH stage.

text: You have an index on { status: 1, price: 1 }. A query is: find({ price: { $lt: 15 } }) — does it use the index?
options: Yes, because price is in the index
No, because the query skips the prefix field (status)
correctAnswer: No, because the query skips the prefix field (status)
explanation: Compound indexes follow the prefix rule: a query must include the leftmost field(s) of the index to use it. { price: { $lt: 15 } } skips status, so MongoDB cannot use this compound index. Create a separate index on price.
hint: The prefix rule — leftmost fields must be included.

text: Create a TTL index that automatically deletes orders 30 days (2592000 seconds) after their 'orderedAt' date:
correctAnswer: expireAfterSeconds
explanation: expireAfterSeconds is the TTL index option. MongoDB runs a background job every 60 seconds to delete documents where the indexed date field is older than expireAfterSeconds from now.
hint: The option is the number of seconds until expiry.

text: Why might MongoDB choose NOT to use an index even when one exists on the queried field?
options: If the index has low selectivity (e.g., boolean field), a full scan may be faster than many random index lookups
Indexes only work with equality queries
Indexes are only used by the aggregation pipeline
correctAnswer: If the index has low selectivity (e.g., boolean field), a full scan may be faster than many random index lookups
explanation: The query planner may choose COLLSCAN over IXSCAN when most documents match the filter. For example, if 90% of books have inStock: true, scanning the index + fetching documents might be slower than just scanning all documents sequentially.
hint: The planner picks the cheaper path.

text:
Write the command to check the execution stats of this query (how many docs were scanned, how long it took):
db.books.find({ authorName: 'George Orwell', inStock: true })
explanation: Chain .explain('executionStats') to any query to see actual execution stats. The 'executionStats' verbosity level executes the query and reports real numbers.
hint: Chain .explain() with the 'executionStats' argument.

text: What is a 'partial index' and when is it useful?
options: An index that covers only some fields of a document
An index that only indexes documents matching a filter expression
An index built while the collection is still receiving writes
An index on a subset of the collection's shards
correctAnswer: An index that only indexes documents matching a filter expression
explanation: A partial index includes only documents matching a filter: db.orders.createIndex({ email: 1 }, { partialFilterExpression: { status: 'active' } }). Smaller than a full index, faster for targeted queries.
hint: It's like a filtered index — only indexes some documents.

text: What does executionStats.totalKeysExamined vs totalDocsExamined tell you?
options: totalKeysExamined = index entries scanned; totalDocsExamined = documents fetched from storage
They are the same thing with different names
totalKeysExamined = unique keys; totalDocsExamined = duplicate docs
Neither is useful — only nReturned matters
correctAnswer: totalKeysExamined = index entries scanned; totalDocsExamined = documents fetched from storage
explanation: When an index is used: totalKeysExamined shows how many index entries were scanned. totalDocsExamined shows how many full documents were loaded. A covered query has totalDocsExamined = 0.
hint: Keys = index entries; Docs = actual documents.

text:
Translate this PostgreSQL index creation:
CREATE INDEX idx_books_author_year ON books (author_id ASC, published_year DESC);
correctAnswer: db.books.createIndex({ authorId: 1, publishedYear: -1 })
explanation: In MongoDB, 1 = ascending (ASC) and -1 = descending (DESC). Compound indexes list all fields in one document object, maintaining their left-to-right order.
hint: 1 = ASC, -1 = DESC in MongoDB.
sqlEquivalent: CREATE INDEX ON books (author_id ASC, published_year DESC)

text: You're building a chat app where messages have { userId, timestamp, text }. You often query 'most recent N messages for a user'. What's the best index?
correctAnswer: { userId: 1, timestamp: -1 }
explanation: ESR rule applied: userId is an equality filter (E), timestamp is a sort field (S) in descending order (most recent first). No range filter needed. This compound index handles both the WHERE and ORDER BY.
hint: Apply the ESR rule: Equality → Sort → Range.

text: What is an 'index intersection' in MongoDB?
options: When two indexes conflict and one is ignored
MongoDB using two separate indexes together to satisfy a single query
The overlap between a text index and a regular index
A compound index covering intersecting fields
correctAnswer: MongoDB using two separate indexes together to satisfy a single query
explanation: Index intersection allows MongoDB to combine results from two single-field indexes. However, in practice, a well-designed compound index almost always outperforms index intersection.
hint: Two indexes working together for one query.

text: createIndex() is called twice with identical parameters on the same collection. What happens?
options: An error is thrown — duplicate index
The second call creates a second, identical index
MongoDB detects the duplicate and the second call is a no-op
correctAnswer: MongoDB detects the duplicate and the second call is a no-op
explanation: createIndex() is idempotent — calling it multiple times with the same definition is safe. MongoDB detects the existing index and returns success without creating a duplicate.
hint: createIndex() is safe to call multiple times.

text: You drop an index that's being actively used by queries. What is the immediate effect?
options: Running queries complete normally; future queries on that field will do collection scans
MongoDB automatically creates a replacement index
An error is thrown — you cannot drop indexes in use
correctAnswer: Running queries complete normally; future queries on that field will do collection scans
explanation: Dropping an index is immediate but doesn't abort in-flight queries. Future queries that relied on the index will fall back to collection scans (slower). MongoDB won't auto-recreate it.
hint: In-flight queries finish; new queries slow down.
